request.question: What consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom that is located in Warrington, England , it has a park that  is now part of the resort, and there are  three locations opened by Gullivers?

title: Amusement park
American Waterfront (Tokyo DisneySea)
Leofoo Village Theme Park
List of former Hersheypark attractions
Gulliver's Land
Safari World
Scandia Amusements
Legoland Windsor Resort
Gulliver's World
Minnesota Zoo
sentences: An amusement park is a park that features various attractions, such as rides and games, as well as other events for entertainment purposes.
A theme park is a type of amusement park that bases its structures and attractions around a central theme, often featuring multiple areas with different themes.
Unlike temporary and mobile funfairs and carnivals, amusement parks are stationary and built for long-lasting operation.
They are more elaborate than city parks and playgrounds, usually providing attractions that cater to a variety of age groups.
While amusement parks often contain themed areas, theme parks place a heavier focus with more intricately-designed themes that revolve around a particular subject or group of subjects.
American Waterfront is a "port-of-call" (themed land) at Tokyo DisneySea in the Tokyo Disney Resort.

It represents the Northeastern seaboard of the United States in the early 20th century, and features two themed areas, an Old Cape Cod section, and a New York Harbor section with an elaborate backstory.
The Leofoo Village Theme Park () is a theme park and a safari located in Guanxi Township, Hsinchu County, Taiwan.

It features three roller coasters, including an inverted shuttle coaster, Screaming Condor, an Intamin twist-and-turn coaster dubbed Sahara Twist, as well as a Vekoma Roller Skater (335m) in the Wild West section of the park, Little Rattler, themed to an old mining railway.
There is also the Nairobi Express, a narrow gauge railway built by Severn Lamb.
The park features many other attractions of different styles and proper themed areas.
Hersheypark (operating as Hershey Park through 1970) is an amusement park located in Hershey, Derry Township, Pennsylvania.
The park was formally opened by Milton S. Hershey on May 30, 1906, and it became an entity of Hershey Estates when the Estates company was established in 1927.
From its opening in 1906 until 1970, the park was an open gate park.
In 1971, the park was gated and an entry fee charged.
This was the first preparations for the renovation project designed by R. Duell and Associates that would begin in 1972.
Since then, the park grew into the park it is today.
Casualties of these different changes are the rides and attractions that were removed or replaced.
Below is a list of the different rides and attractions Hersheypark has removed, which includes kiddie rides, thrill rides, roller coasters, themed areas, funhouses and other attractions of amusement.
Gulliver's Land is a children's theme park in Milton Keynes, England.
It opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's.
(The other two parks are Gulliver's World and Gulliver's Kingdom).
The park has been designed especially for families.
The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort, providing themed accommodation and short break packages.
Gulliver's Land resort includes Splash Zone, NERF Zone, The SFEAR and Dinosaur and Farm Park.
Safari World is a tourist attraction in Bangkok, Thailand that consists of two parks named Marine Park and Safari Park, operated by Safari World Public Limited.
The park was opened in 1988 with a total area of 480 acres for its open zoo and 180 acres for its bird park.

A major renovation to enhance effectiveness of land use began on 17 April 1989 and its total area developed for the leisure park now consists of an open zoo and a marine park on 500 rai (approximately 200 acres) of land.

Scandia Amusements is a chain of Scandinavian-themed family amusement centers in California, including three locations: Scandia Fun Center in Sacramento, Scandia Family Fun Center in Victorville, and Scandia Amusement Park in Ontario.
All three locations are located adjacent to a major freeway, and include attractions such as an arcade, "Baltic Sea Bumper Boats", batting cages, miniature golf, and "Stockholm Raceway".

Scandia Amusement Park in Ontario is the only location to include a full amusement park, including two roller coasters, called the "Scandia Screamer" and "Little Screamer", along with twelve other amusement rides.
Legoland Windsor Resort, also known as Legoland Windsor, is a child-orentated theme park and resort in Windsor, Berkshire in England, themed around the Lego toy system.
The park opened in 1996 on the former Windsor Safari Park site as the second Legoland after Legoland Billund in Denmark.
In common with the other Legolands across the world, the park's attractions consist of a mixture of Lego-themed rides, models, and building workshops.
The park was acquired by Merlin Entertainments in 2005, which now operates the park, with the Lego Group retaining part ownership (30%).
The facilities are mainly targeted at children between three and twelve.
Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England.
Gulliver’s World theme park consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom.
The park features rides and attractions that have been designed especially for children between the ages of 2 and 13.
Splash Zone and NERF Zone are two indoor attractions located next to the main theme park.
The Minnesota Zoo (formerly the Minnesota Zoological Garden), is an AZA-accredited zoo in Apple Valley, Minnesota.
When it opened on May 22, 1978<ref name="mnzoo.org/timeline">http://www.mnzoo.org/guests/birthday/index.asp retrieved October 22, 2006</ref> it was fairly revolutionary in its exhibit design.
The zoo, built in a suburbanizing rural area, had more space to house exhibits and was one of the first zoos to organize its animals by their living environment as opposed to their species.
Exhibits are arrayed in six themed areas, including three themed walking trails ranging from one to two miles in length:

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: What consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom that is located in Warrington, England , it has a park that  is now part of the resort, and there are  three locations opened by Gullivers?
hop1_docs: Gulliver's World | Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England.  Gulliver’s World theme park consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom.  The park features rides and attractions that have been designed especially for children between the ages of 2 and 13.  Splash Zone and NERF Zone are two indoor attractions located next to the main theme park.

Gulliver's Land | Gulliver's Land is a children's theme park in Milton Keynes, England.  It opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's.  (The other two parks are Gulliver's World and Gulliver's Kingdom).  The park has been designed especially for families.  The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort, providing themed accommodation and short break packages.  Gulliver's Land resort includes Splash Zone, NERF Zone, The SFEAR and Dinosaur and Farm Park.

Gulliver's Kingdom | Gulliver's Kingdom (also known as Gulliver's Matlock Bath) is a theme park aimed at children aged 3 to 13 in the Derbyshire town of Matlock Bath, England.  Founded in 1978 by Ray Phillips, it is still owned by the Phillips family and now has sister theme parks; Gulliver's World in Warrington and Gulliver's Land in Milton Keynes, which opened in 1989 and 1999 respectively.  The park was originally created by Ray Phillips as a model village for his young children to enjoy, and it is close to the site of the Victorian Switchback rollercoaster ride (after which the theme park's main rollercoaster was named).

Legoland Windsor Resort | Legoland Windsor Resort, also known as Legoland Windsor, is a child-orentated theme park and resort in Windsor, Berkshire in England, themed around the Lego toy system.  The park opened in 1996 on the former Windsor Safari Park site as the second Legoland after Legoland Billund in Denmark.  In common with the other Legolands across the world, the park's attractions consist of a mixture of Lego-themed rides, models, and building workshops.  The park was acquired by Merlin Entertainments in 2005, which now operates the park, with the Lego Group retaining part ownership (30%).  The facilities are mainly targeted at children between three and twelve.

Safari World | Safari World is a tourist attraction in Bangkok, Thailand that consists of two parks named Marine Park and Safari Park, operated by Safari World Public Limited.  The park was opened in 1988 with a total area of 480 acres for its open zoo and 180 acres for its bird park.  A major renovation to enhance effectiveness of land use began on 17 April 1989 and its total area developed for the leisure park now consists of an open zoo and a marine park on 500 rai (approximately 200 acres) of land.

Lilliput Nunataks | The Lilliput Nunataks ( ) are three nunataks, from 600 to high and trending southeast–northwest, located 3 nmi north of Gulliver Nunatak on the east side of Graham Land, Antarctica.  The nunataks are snow free on their southeast sides.  They were charted by the Falkland Islands Dependencies Survey and photographed from the air by the Ronne Antarctic Research Expedition in 1947.  The name, from Jonathan Swift's "Gulliver's Travels", means land of small people and was applied by the UK Antarctic Place-Names Committee in association with Gulliver Nunatak.

Disney's Fort Wilderness Resort & Campground | Disney's Fort Wilderness Resort & Campground is a themed camping resort located in the Magic Kingdom Resort Area at the Walt Disney World Resort.  It officially opened on November 19, 1971.  The resort is adjacent to Bay Lake and Disney's River Country, a now-defunct water park.  The resort is also located near Disney's Wilderness Lodge.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Gulliver's World — passage 1: "Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England" and "consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom."
  - Gulliver's Land — passage 2: "Gulliver's Land is a children's theme park in Milton Keynes" ; "opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's." ; "The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort."
  - Gulliver's Kingdom — passage 3: "Gulliver's Kingdom ... founded in 1978" ; passage 3 also lists the other two parks ("Gulliver's World in Warrington and Gulliver's Land in Milton Keynes").
  - No single passage explicitly ties the "park that is now part of the resort" to the Warrington (Gulliver's World) page.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - The question conflates facts from different pages: the Warrington themed areas (Gulliver's World) vs. the "now part of the resort" phrasing which appears on Gulliver's Land.
  - It's unclear which specific entity the question asks for (Gulliver's World, Gulliver's Land, or the Gulliver parks brand).
  - Missing explicit statement that the Warrington park "has a park that is now part of the resort" (no passage links that resort-note to Warrington).

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Need confirmation whether the "park that is now part of the resort" refers to Gulliver's World (Warrington) or to Gulliver's Land (Milton Keynes).
  - Need an authoritative summary/list showing all Gulliver parks and which features (resort/adventurers village) belong to which site.
  - If the question expects "the third opened by Gulliver's," need the mapping of opening order to park names/dates.

- retrieval_direction (priority order):
  1. Open "Gulliver's World" page (full article) — confirm themed areas and any resort/park/resort-adjacent mentions.
  2. Open "Gulliver's Land" page — confirm "Adventurers Village is now part of the resort" and that it is the third park opened (1999).
  3. Open "Gulliver's Kingdom" page — confirm founding year and relation among the three parks.
  4. Search/query: "List of Gulliver's theme parks" or "Gulliver's parks list opening years Gulliver's World Gulliver's Land Gulliver's Kingdom" to get a consolidated statement tying which features belong to which park.
response.hop2_query: Gulliver's parks list Gulliver's World Warrington Gulliver's Land "Adventurers Village" "now part of the resort" opening years
hop2_docs: Gulliver's Land | Gulliver's Land is a children's theme park in Milton Keynes, England.  It opened in 1999 and is the third park to be opened by Gulliver's.  (The other two parks are Gulliver's World and Gulliver's Kingdom).  The park has been designed especially for families.  The Adventurers Village is now part of the resort, providing themed accommodation and short break packages.  Gulliver's Land resort includes Splash Zone, NERF Zone, The SFEAR and Dinosaur and Farm Park.

Gulliver's Kingdom | Gulliver's Kingdom (also known as Gulliver's Matlock Bath) is a theme park aimed at children aged 3 to 13 in the Derbyshire town of Matlock Bath, England.  Founded in 1978 by Ray Phillips, it is still owned by the Phillips family and now has sister theme parks; Gulliver's World in Warrington and Gulliver's Land in Milton Keynes, which opened in 1989 and 1999 respectively.  The park was originally created by Ray Phillips as a model village for his young children to enjoy, and it is close to the site of the Victorian Switchback rollercoaster ride (after which the theme park's main rollercoaster was named).

Gulliver's World | Gulliver's World is a theme park located in Warrington, England.  Gulliver’s World theme park consists of multiple themed areas including Lilliput Land, Gully Town and Safari Kingdom.  The park features rides and attractions that have been designed especially for children between the ages of 2 and 13.  Splash Zone and NERF Zone are two indoor attractions located next to the main theme park.

Glumdalclitch | Glumdalclitch is the name Gulliver gives his "nurse" in Book II of Jonathan Swift's "Gulliver's Travels".  In Book I, Gulliver travels to the land of Lilliput.  Leaving there, he travels to the land of Brobdingnag.  In Lilliput, Gulliver was a giant, and in Brobdingnag, he is a dwarf, with the proportions reversed.

The Adventures of Gulliver | The Adventures of Gulliver is a 1968 television cartoon produced by Hanna-Barbera Productions.  The show is based on the novel "Gulliver's Travels".  The show aired Saturday mornings on ABC-TV and lasted for one season in its original broadcast.

Gulliver's Travels | Gulliver's Travels, whose full title is Travels into Several Remote Nations of the World.  In Four Parts.  By Lemuel Gulliver, First a Surgeon, and then a Captain of Several Ships, (1726, amended 1735), is a prose satire by Irish writer and clergyman Jonathan Swift, that is both a satire on human nature and the "travellers' tales" literary subgenre.  It is Swift's best known full-length work, and a classic of English literature.  He himself claimed that he wrote "Gulliver's Travels" "to vex the world rather than divert it".

Brobdingnag | Brobdingnag is a fictional land in Jonathan Swift's satirical novel "Gulliver's Travels" occupied by giants.  Lemuel Gulliver visits the land after the ship on which he is travelling is blown off course and he is separated from a party exploring the unknown land.  In the second preface to the book, Gulliver laments that this is a misspelling introduced by the publisher and the land is actually called Brobdingrag.
